response:
source The implementation begins by initializing two accumulator variables, one to track the running sum of squares and another to maintain the product of cubes, ensuring they start with the correct identity values for an empty set.

***

We iterate through each integer provided in the input slice, calculating the square of the current number and adding it to our sum accumulator, which naturally handles the zero case for an empty list.

***

Simultaneously within the same loop, we compute the cube of the current number and multiply it into our product accumulator, relying on the mathematical property that multiplying by one preserves the identity for an empty sequence.

***

If the input slice contains no elements, the loop body never executes, leaving the sum at zero and the product at one, which perfectly satisfies the specified edge case requirements without needing explicit conditional checks.

***

After processing all items in the collection, we return a fixed-size array containing the final calculated sum of squares as the first element and the final product of cubes as the second element.

***

This approach ensures efficient single-pass traversal of the data structure while maintaining numerical precision and adhering strictly to the defined output format for both populated and empty input sets.
